text.fullText: From: singingalltheway@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/15/2026 12:20:50 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ----------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from singingalltheway@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, The Great Lakes, the greatest system of surface freshwater in the world, rely on wetlands, rivers, and streams to filter pollution, prevent flooding in our communities, and support fishing and recreation. It is critical that the U.S. EPA sticks to its mission to enforce environmental laws and defend, not degrade, the Clean Water Act. The proposed rule change to Section 401 seeks to take control away from states and Tribes, thus loosening the clean water protections strongly supported by most Americans regardless of political affiliation. In particular: - Changing this part of the Clean Water Act betrays the idea of federal and state cooperation, which is foundational to America's system of government. - This limits states' and Tribes' opportunities to prioritize their specific needs to protect their water. - Weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protections. - Shortening project reviews does not allow states and Tribes adequate time to thoroughly assess projects that may increase pollution. I am especially concerned that further weakening of clean water protections will harm waters in the Great Lakes, a vital resource that tens of millions of residents depend upon for drinking water and our economy. Such a change will likely have a cascading effect, eventually harming sources of freshwater around the country. This proposed rule change will further strip protections of wetlands, which provide significant regional economic benefits, such as: - Supporting recreational activities that bring in $50 billion annually to the region - Filtering out pollution to keep Great Lakes water clean for drinking and recreation - Controlling flooding, reducing damage and costs to communities and homes For the reasons of popular national support, regional economic implications, the official mission of the U.S. EPA, and in support of states and Tribal rights, I urge you to fully enforce the Clean Water Act and maintain the authority of states and Tribes to protect clean water. Sincerely, Marianne Watertown, MA
